posts.cooked:

That’s what I’ve understood about 2012, more of a transition year for Charles, with a bigger role in 2013.

However, the drinkability of 2012 reds in general (not Arnoux in particular) seems surprisingly good right now for 1ers and below. Much more expressive than 2010’s.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Kristoffer B r y d e r:

Are you saying, despite all the praise 2015 is getting at the moment, that you prefer the 2016? I wonder if the whole cluster thing is what’s wrong with my 2012’s, since the complete de-stemming of their older vintages was probably what made them appeal to me.

If the discussion is limited to the Arnoux-Lachaux Bourgogne “Pinot Fin”, yes, I prefer the '16 to the '15, for the reasons given.

Charles is really performing a complete volte face here, with much gentler extraction and much less new oak in the cellar, and much more attentive, less chemical viticulture. The idea is to get naturally very concentrated grapes and then extract gently, rather than getting overcropped grapes with poor chemistry and compensating with extraction, oak and acid-adds in the cuverie. One of his big influences is Leroy, and he is now training the domaine’s vines like Lalou’s team does (an expensive and laborious undertaking). You can’t transform vineyards overnight, nor do you master the technical dimensions of whole cluster fermentation in one or two vintages, so naturally there will be a learning curve, but the rationale and the integrity of what Charles is trying to do are unimpeachable, and the qualitative progression already evident. He also happens to have some nice old vines with good genetics which should make things move forward a bit faster.

posts.name: Richard Frankel
posts.cooked:
Kristoffer B r y d e r:

Anyone tried the Pinot fin? The ‘09 and ‘10 put the domain on my radar, although the ‘12 has been consistently underwhelming. I suspect the characteristics of 2015 will suit this wine well.

I tasted the ‘13 (with Charles in his cellar, another story!) and found it straightforward, big, red fruited and very enjoyable. I thought it a lovely wine, and a great entry into the developing Arnoux style. ‘14s in my cellar as yet untouched, ‘15 on the way.

posts.name: N@th@n V@nd€rgrift
posts.cooked: Why is it greed to price something at market rates? No one has a problem when “the market” pushes prices down for less heralded vintages, but it’s a problem when prices rise for vintages that people are chasing more vigorously? Retailers don’t get to just buy big vintages, they have to buy every vintage in order to maintain allocations. Then they have to figure out a way to sell less desirable vintages even though the buying public is only interested in 1 vintage in 4. If you think about other kinds of goods, I’m surprised it isn’t worse.

posts.name: Rauno E N G el
posts.cooked: Fair point Nathan. And in the absence of a better way of allocating bottles to end-consumers, market rates are pretty fair !

posts.name: Robert Panzer
posts.cooked:
I have represented Arnoux since '07.
Charles was told by his dad Pasacal in ‘12: go into the office and make your vinification plan. The keys are yours. So he was very much in the driver’s seat with dad riding shotgun. Now dad is not even riding sidecar, but is around if he has questions, which Charles’ increasingly honed convictions seeminly possessing not much doubt. His homies (Bouley/Nico Rossignol/Lamy) are more likely to be conferred with.
I don’t necessarily agree that his '16s are any sort of noteworthy improvement over his '15s. He made great wines in both vintages, in the style of each year, with sime flip floppy differences in different appellations. (One year the Pagets is better than another, etc…yeah I know, the trophy hunters are all over the Pagets!).
The '15 Pinot Fin is yum yum with a side of yum.
Yeah, $350 and $950 is speculator town.

posts.name: Max Kelly
posts.cooked: So here’s the question: as the price for Arnoux’s Suchots enters boost phase, who are the alternatives to whom we should turn? I’ve had some very good Cathiard Suchots, but they’re not exactly cheap either. I’ve read distinctly mixed reviews of the wines from Hudelot-Noellat (despite the old vine holdings and corresponding prices), Grivot, and l’Arlot. Both Lamarche and Domaine de Bellene have bottlings as well, but I don’t see them getting a lot of play. Curious to hear what others think.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Max K:

So here’s the question: as the price for Arnoux’s Suchots enters boost phase, who are the alternatives to whom we should turn? I’ve had some very good Cathiard Suchots, but they’re not exactly cheap either. I’ve read distinctly mixed reviews of the wines from Hudelot-Noellat (despite the old vine holdings and corresponding prices), Grivot, and l’Arlot. Both Lamarche and Domaine de Bellene have bottlings as well, but I don’t see them getting a lot of play. Curious to hear what others think.

Do we actually like Suchots that much? I tend to find it structurally on the rustic side, with boisterous dark fruit. (Of course, there are some notable differences within the (quite large) vineyard as far as drainage is concerned). To me, it is priced more by zip code than by what’s in the bottle (e.g. from Hudelot-Noellat it’s the same price as the much superior Clos Vougeot), though I would love to have my mind changed.

I think the example from Berthaut-Gerbet is likely to get pretty good as Amelie brings the Gerbet vineyards into better shape.

Michel Noellat is one of the largest holders, it’s a pity they don’t do a better job.

posts.name: Max Kelly
posts.cooked:
Heresy! Alan will be by any moment with a pitchfork and torches.

More seriously - I can’t claim a ton of experience with it, but I’ve enjoyed a number of vintages of Arnoux and Cathiard. I associate it with black cherry/raspberry, some spice, and a very silky texture.

When I started collecting, Arnoux’s Suchots was one of the few widely lauded VR 1ers that was not prohibitively expensive (e.g. Cros Parentoux, Brûlées, Petits Monts) and reasonably available (as opposed to Les Rouges, La Croix Rameau, Reignots, etc.) Perhaps I followed Coates’ hierarchy too closely.

And more to the point - what VR premier crus do you like better? And don’t say Cros Parantoux. I have noticed that Reignots doesn’t seem to reach the U.S. market at all anymore, and commands higher prices than Suchots in the European and Asian markets.

posts.cooked:

Both Lamarche and Domaine de Bellene have bottlings as well, but I don’t see them getting a lot of play. Curious to hear what others think.

Do we actually like Suchots that much? I tend to find it structurally on the rustic side, with boisterous dark fruit. (Of course, there are some notable differences within the (quite large) vineyard as far as drainage is concerned). To me, it is priced more by zip code than by what’s in the bottle (e.g. from Hudelot-Noellat it’s the same price as the much superior Clos Vougeot), though I would love to have my mind changed.

I think the example from Berthaut-Gerbet is likely to get pretty good as Amelie brings the Gerbet vineyards into better shape.

Michel Noellat is one of the largest holders, it’s a pity they don’t do a better job.

Do we really like Suchots that much? Sounds like a great tasting waiting to happen. I’d like to taste more of it, and with age. I do recall the 12’ Hudelot-Noellat Suchots at La Paulee some years back being quite the stand out, and among the best wines there.

Lots of variability in Suchots due to the producers being so varied in their style and evolutions. Seems Arnoux-Lachaux and Hudelot-Noellat may be at the forefront for now, while Cathiard, Grivot, and Liger-Belair also figure to lurk near the top. Confuron-Cotetidot, L’Arlot, and Jadot are not the most fashionable but appear squarely in the mix.

As you say, Berthaut-Gerbet would be one to follow. Curious about the Petits Monts as well.

posts.name: Rauno E N G el
posts.cooked: Max - I think Beaumonts is at least as good as Suchots. Sure, none of these VR 1ers are cheap anymore, but it’s usually less than the Malconsorts or Suchots from a particular producer. And I often prefer it.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Rauno E (NZ):

Max - I think Beaumonts is at least as good as Suchots. Sure, none of these VR 1ers are cheap anymore, but it’s usually less than the Malconsorts or Suchots from a particular producer. And I often prefer it.

Agreed. Beaux Monts is quite large and consequently varies meaningfully, some parts giving wines that are more ‘solaire’ and ripe, others more tense and mineral, but at its best it is fabulous.

posts.name: Max Kelly
posts.cooked:
William Kelley:
Rauno E (NZ):

Max - I think Beaumonts is at least as good as Suchots. Sure, none of these VR 1ers are cheap anymore, but it’s usually less than the Malconsorts or Suchots from a particular producer. And I often prefer it.

Agreed. Beaux Monts is quite large and consequently varies meaningfully, some parts giving wines that are more ‘solaire’ and ripe, others more tense and mineral, but at its best it is fabulous.

I agree - have had great wines from Hudelot-Noellat and Grivot. Recently saw Confuron-Gindre for a relatively reasonable price in my market, but grabbed a village VR to try first. I know both Leroy and Dujac make very pricy Beaumonts. Any other makers stand out here?

Malconsorts has gotten lots of ink here recently, although I wonder whether Lamarche’s rather affordable version is showing the same marked improvement as some of their other wines.

What about some of the other vineyards?

  • Petits Monts (I was recently very impressed by Drouhin’s '09 and '10, but once again they are pricy)
  • Chaumes (seems like it’s an afterthought)
  • Brûlées (does anyone other than Leroy and Meo make wine worth hunting down?)
  • Reignots (I know Liger-Belair is considered the benchmark, but it’s impossible to find from almost any maker these days)
  • La Croix Rameau (I had a pretty nice '10 Lamarche earlier this year, but no idea whether the other producers are worth trying)
  • Les Rouges (hardly ever mentioned)

posts.name: Marcus Dean
posts.cooked: dont forget Les Orveaux, another rarely mentioned Vosne premier, I quite enjoyed the 14 Mongeard (although the caveat with his wines are they require a fair bit of sleep time to show their best).

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Max K:

What about some of the other vineyards?

  • Petits Monts (I was recently very impressed by Drouhin’s '09 and '10, but once again they are pricy)
  • Chaumes (seems like it’s an afterthought)
  • Brûlées (does anyone other than Leroy and Meo make wine worth hunting down?)
  • Reignots (I know Liger-Belair is considered the benchmark, but it’s impossible to find from almost any maker these days)
  • La Croix Rameau (I had a pretty nice '10 Lamarche earlier this year, but no idea whether the other producers are worth trying)
  • Les Rouges (hardly ever mentioned)

Cécile Tremblay makes a nice Beaux Monts. It seems otiose to mention Emmanuel Rouget’s.

Petit Monts is usually one of Maxime’s best wines at Georges Noellat.

Chaumes is often so facile that it is hard to take it seriously as a 1er cru. I’d rather drink Mugneret-Gibourg Vosne village.

Brûlées is arguably Méo’s best wine. Bruno Clavelier also makes a lovely rendition.

Reignots - Felettig is quite under the radar, think it all goes to Asia though. It used to be blended into the Vosne 1er cru. Cathiard’s vines here are young.

Croix Rameau - not that I know of, and I do not really have an idea of what this vineyard’s identity is

Les Rouges - Tremblay, Grivot, but let’s not talk about this!

Great tips, William. La Croix Rameau sits a the bottom of RSV, and is reputed to echo (whether faintly or strongly is debatable) that vineyard’s elegance and perfume. The lone bottle of Lamarche that I’ve tasted is just one data point, but it supported that reputation.

posts.name: A Songeur
posts.cooked:
Brulees : Grivot and Clavelier also make good ones (I have not had Eugenie but Engel was also great)
Beaumonts: Grivot and Hudelot Noellat also make good wine
Otherwise, agree with other inputs.
Suchots HN has increased a lot in price…

Marcus Dean:

Oh and please, any of you that have tasted the Arnoux Chaumes 2015, can I have your thoughts please, I have a case coming after hearing very good things, then saw AMs note which declared it faulty?

2015 Arnoux-Lachaux, Vosne-Romanée 1er Les Chaumes: A wider nose (than Nuits Clos des Corvées Pagets), still some aromatic vibration, cooler fruit. Ooh, still some texture from tannin but not dry. A classic Vosne that’s beautifully proportioned. (Tasted 09 November 2016 from barrel)

Reignots is the favourite wine of Charles Lachaux from his line-up and for those with the best of contacts, the two (with Liger-Belair) will offer great comparisons in the future.
The Felettig Reignots is sometimes not made due to low yields, instead being blended with Petits Monts and Chaumes - they have 2 small old-vines parcels in Reignots - 2009 delivered 3 barrels. In total they have 0.095 ha.
Beaumonts - Daniel Rion makes a lovely version - just give it 10 years.
The Chaumes of Liger- Belair is very fine these days.
Petits Monts, it’s a negoce wine, but the bottling by Cyprien Arlaud is super. Of-course Amélie Berthaut’s wine will be the one to watch and to compare to those of Drouhin and Liger-Belair.
Brulées - I have a soft spot for the wine of Bruno Clavelier - and who knows what the wine of Eugenie tastes like these days, or even where it goes - those wines seem lost in a black hole…

posts.name: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)
posts.cooked:
William Kelley:

Do we actually like Suchots that much? I tend to find it structurally on the rustic side, with boisterous dark fruit…

Seriously, rusticity is the last I´m thinking of when talking about Suchots … a good Suchots is elegant, balanced, with dark-red berry and cherry fruit, often even silky and with sweet tannins, but with less spices and sometimes lacking depth and drama …


Robert Pavlovich:

… Seems Arnoux-Lachaux and Hudelot-Noellat may be at the forefront for now, while Cathiard, Grivot, and Liger-Belair also figure to lurk near the top. Confuron-Cotetidot, L’Arlot, and Jadot are not the most fashionable but appear squarely in the mix.
…

Are you kidding? Liger-Belair is absolutely on top … you can argue if LB or Arnoux is THE best, but all other producers are well behind (incl. Hudelot-Noellat) … and at Cathiard Suchots is not the best Cru (this is Malconsorts).

Reg.Chaumes: a Chaumes is quite on the fruity side, with a lot of raspberries, very attractive, earlier drinkable, but not one of the most serious 1ers in Vosne …
posts.display_username: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)

posts.name: A Songeur
posts.cooked:
Gerhardt,
While I agree Arnoux has the best plot and vines,
LB and Arnoux had a reputation for a heavy hand with wood. Has it changed? Or is it to your taste?

posts.name: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)
posts.cooked:
A Songeur:

Gerhardt,
While I agree Arnoux has the best plot and vines,
LB and Arnoux had a reputation for a heavy hand with wood. Has it changed? Or is it to your taste?

I have never tasted a top MATURE Suchots with (too much) oak … only when too young the oak component is noticable …
… so if it´s oaky it´s too young …

2006 was the 1st Suchots vintage of Liger-Belair … still very youthful …

posts.name: A Songeur
posts.cooked:
Thanks

What do you think of Dominique Laurent Suchots? He claims he has grapes from best parcel and is applying “a bit of wood”
And Confuron Cotetidot Suchots? he has the biggest plot and uses stems and very ripe grapes?
I bought both producers but not long enough ago so have not tasted them.

posts.name: Robert Panzer
posts.cooked:
Laurent has fantastically old vine Suchots that ages wonderfully, like almost all Laurent wines…
The Cotetidot (the largest Suchots langholder at 2ha+, from plots high and low) is one of the appellations best, but the house style of systematic 100% whole cluster demands lots of time in bottle.
The Millot Suchots is terrific, from fairly only vines.
I echo the here here for Clavelier (everything he makes!) and Grivot Suchots. I was lucky enough to import a good bit of Grivot Suchots in '07/'08 (mags too!), which are sort of unicorn wines.
The Hudelot Suchots, imho, is worth every penny. That stuff is sicko sicko good, a Grand cru in all but name. (the price is sort of Grand cru-ish, but in Vosne, a bargain of a Grand cru at that). The oldest vines in Vosne, apparently?
The Michel Noellat Suchots can be better in some vintages than others, but does often leave much to be desired. Whereas their Boudots, from 80+ year old vines, is consistently a very very good wine that, like all great Boudots, goes overlooked due to the label licker phenomenon moreso than any qualitative assessment. It is terrific.
For Petit Monts: SIRUGUE. One of the largest land holders there, the last few vintages have been knockout good to my taste, all on airy fine delicacy (which, broadly speaking, defines the house style), with young generation Arnaud Sirugue doing very nice things there. Amélie Berthaut makes a killer PMonts as well, although she needs to get the oak in greater harmony, as a general rule, for her top cuvées (imho).
For Chaumes: Cheurlin (G Noellat) and Charles Lachaux (Arnoux) would be my first choices.
The '15 and '16 Arnoux Chaumes are both plain terrific, as Charles continues to really recognize what each climat needs in order to shine. The '16 Pagets is stunning in its finesse, for example, whereas Pascal would often make it “harder/firmer”…
As to Orveaux and Brulées, I have a sneaky under-the-radar new discovery (for me) player in mind, but will wait to reveal until my allocation of '16s is dialed in. ;0

Disclaimer: I represent Arnoux/Sirugue/G Noellat/Clavelier/Hudelot/M Noellat (I think such disclaimers are unneccessary, but will try to appease the wolf pack).

posts.name: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)
posts.cooked:
A Songeur:

Thanks

What do you think of Dominique Laurent Suchots? He claims he has grapes from best parcel and is applying “a bit of wood”
And Confuron Cotetidot Suchots? he has the biggest plot and uses stems and very ripe grapes?
I bought both producers but not long enough ago so have not tasted them.

I´ve had D.Laurent Suchots from late 90ies and 2000 - but not from later. These were excellent with depth and beautifully balanced wood …

Confuron Cotetidot: a quite traditional producer with a lot of stems (maybe too much?) … the wines need a lot of time - I don´t think it´s a very refined style, old fashioned, certainly good, but not my fav. style … however the youngest vintage I´ve tasted was (I think) 2003 (too young) …

posts.cooked:
Robert Panzer:

…
I echo the here here for Clavelier (everything he makes!) and Grivot Suchots. …

Do you mean Clavelier makes a Suchots? I don´t think so …
He´s got Beaumonts and Brulees … (very good indeed)

I was never a great Grivot-fan … they have a very distinctive (cold soaked dark berried) house style which is (imho) not a textbook of elegance - but that´s personal …
However - in the mid to late 90ies they hold the record of corked bottles … so far as our joke is “it´s corked … so it might be Grivot …” )

Robert Panzer:

…
For Chaumes: Cheurlin (G Noellat) and Charles Lachaux (Arnoux) would be my first choices.
The '15 and '16 Arnoux Chaumes are both plain terrific, as Charles continues to really recognize what each climat needs in order to shine. The '16 Pagets is stunning in its finesse, for example, whereas Pascal would often make it “harder/firmer”…
As to Orveaux and Brulées, I have a sneaky under-the-radar new discovery (for me) player in mind, but will wait to reveal until my allocation of '16s is dialed in. ;0

Disclaimer: I represent Arnoux/Sirugue/G Noellat/Clavelier/Hudelot/M Noellat (I think such disclaimers are unneccessary, but will try to appease the wolf pack).

The best Chaumes I´ve ever tasted was from L-M Liger-Belair … Arnoux is also very fine (but Suchots and Reignots are simply more serious … it´s simply the terroir).
I´ve had an outstanding Chaumes 1996 from Confuron-Gindre, really very fine … but the wines need serious time, 2008-2015 are all far too young … and I haven´t tasted anything in between …

I think your disclaimer is spot on … I would leave it like that …

posts.name: A Songeur
posts.cooked:
Thanks all! Used to buy Hudelot Noellat (and a bit of Grivot) when available and love them (but became scarce and pricey), bought Confuron Cot (and HN) in 2010 only and Dom Laurent since then so too early to taste… although maybe 2011 soon?

I went very long in 2015 with Vosne 1er (I have to stop as I age…) with a few bottles of many 1er

  • D Laurent Suchots, Beaumonts et Reignots, Nat Vigot Petits Monts, Forey Petits Monts et Gaudichots, Lamarche Croix Rameau, Bichot Malconsorts, Confuron Gindre Chaumes and Brulees

Difficulty is that producers’ styles are so different it will be difficult to identify terroirs.

posts.name: R Nanda
posts.cooked:
Had the opportunity to taste at the domaine with Charles on Tuesday morning. We tried the 16s (Bourgogne and Village wines) out of tank, and a handful of 15s out of bottle. A few thoughts:

1 - I really like what I tasted in 16’s across Burgundy, including at Arnoux. The Bourgogne and Village NSD and Vosne HM all seemed to punch above their weight.

2 - The 15s we tasted were also quite good, but the vintage signature is clear – bigger riper, blacker fruit and structure to match. The wines are not shutdown by any means, but it will take years and years for the terroir to shine though.

3 - The 15 Suchots is the real deal. One of the better wines that I tasted across 15 domaines visited. Is it a buy at $350? That’s an interesting debate. But it strikes me as a Grand Cru quality wine, which is the priciest of territories in Vosne.

My very brief tasting notes are on CT.

only tasted there once, about 15 years ago. Suchots was and still is the real deal.

Tasted there on Thursday, and was reminded how great - and forgotten - the Suchots is today at Arlot. Almost a grainy Premeaux tannin in 2016, but multidimensional wine - and the tannin will be but a memory as these start drinking. Great stuff - RSV no slouch either…

The RSV is (maybe and not always) 1-2 points “better”, but this is almost 3 times the price …

posts.name: Marcus Dean
posts.cooked:
Just cracked a 2015 Arnoux Bourgogne Pinot Fin,
What a cracking wine, you can taste the whole cluster but the stalks are ripe and add a floating mid palate sensation to the wine, only just managing to hit mid weight with a great balance and a very very fine tannin finish. A Spring or Autumn wine. Drink it now before it shuts down or look at it again in 2026. Me Likey
